evidence_quotes.quote: Currently the most affected medium for this is video games, where entire games are being removed and completely lost due to the developer/publisher/seller/whoever deciding to simply end the service.
consumers not having guarantees on retaining digital goods they paid for in full.

paragraphs.text: As part of the Digital Fairness Act I believe it is prudent to consider the concept of consumers not having guarantees on retaining digital goods they paid for in full. Many digital good such as software, apps, video games, etc all come with a "license" that allows the creator of said content to apply any rules onto, be it revoking said license and thus access to the digital goods for any reason, or no reason at all, during the operation and support timeline of whatever digital content that was sold. This also allows the creators to completely revoke access and remove from all platforms the digital content that was sold to a consumer when the seller simply no longer wishes to support it anymore. Currently the most affected medium for this is video games, where entire games are being removed and completely lost due to the developer/publisher/seller/whoever deciding to simply end the service. While indefinite support of a license of a digital product is of course not expected by anyone, this should definitely be looked into and options provided to consumers. Either allowing the consumer(s) to continue using said digital content with no further updates from the creator going forward by providing a way to keep it doing, make the digital content public and available to everyone in whatever state it is left at the point of abandonment or advising of the exact timeline the digital content (or support for it) will "last" until, so correct expectations can be established at the point of sale.
